title: SpaceX IPO, Oracle earnings, May CPI: What to watch this week
content:
Asking for a Trend Host Josh Lipton previews several of the biggest stories to come throughout next week, including SpaceX's (SPAX.PVT) long-awaited mega-IPO, earnings from Adobe (ADBE) and Oracle (ORCL), and the latest inflation data in the form of May's Consumer Price Index (CPI) report.

Video Transcript

00:03 Speaker A

Time now for to watch in the week of June 8th. It's a busy week for investors with insights and earnings from tech as well as fresh inflation data. But all eyes will be on SpaceX's highly anticipated IPO. Report suggests the company is targeting a valuation of around 1.75 trillion dollars, potentially making it one of the largest market debuts in the history. On Friday, SpaceX is targeting an IPO price of 135 per share. We'll trade on the Nasdaq under the ticker symbol SPCX.

00:32 Speaker A

Switching gears now to inflation, investors will be closely watching Wednesday's consumer price index or CPI, economists expecting price pressures to remain relatively contained with inflation cooling on the month over month basis.

00:46 Speaker A

Turning to tech, Oracle reporting earnings on Wednesday, investors expecting an update on demand for the company's cloud infrastructure business as companies ramp up spending on AI.

00:58 Speaker A

And rounding out the week, Adobe is reporting quarterly results on Thursday and also expecting good news about Firefly, Adobe's AI software. Investors will also be focusing on the company's AI strategy and whether new generative AI features will help drive customer growth.

View Comments

title: Inflation data, SpaceX IPO to test markets after broad selloff
content:
[Wall street sign in New York with New York Stock Exchange background]
naphtalina/iStock via Getty Images

Investors head into the week ahead facing a difficult backdrop after a sharp market selloff rattled stocks, bonds, gold and cryptocurrencies, leaving few traditional havens untouched, The Wall Street Journal reported Sunday.

Friday's stronger-than-expected U.S. jobs report sparked a broad reassessment of interest-rate expectations. The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/COMP:IND]) fell 4.2%, Treasury yields climbed to their highest levels since early 2025 and traders increased bets that the Federal Reserve could raise rates before year-end. Gold (XAUUSD:CUR [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/XAUUSD:CUR]) also slid toward its lowest levels of 2026.

The focus now shifts to two major tests in the coming days: a closely watched inflation report and the debut of SpaceX's (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) record-setting initial public offering. Both events could influence investor sentiment at a time when markets are already grappling with higher borrowing costs and concerns about stretched valuations.

Some Wall Street veterans warned that Friday's decline exposed vulnerabilities created by the market's heavy reliance on a small group of artificial intelligence-related stocks. Bridgewater Associates founder Ray Dalio said rising bond yields and lofty equity valuations have made fixed-income investments more competitive, raising questions about the sustainability of the market's rally. He also cautioned that speculative enthusiasm surrounding AI shares resembles characteristics seen in past market bubbles.

Technology stocks bore the brunt of the selling. Semiconductor shares lost more than $1 trillion in market value over two sessions, although the sector remains sharply higher for the year. Investors had been counting on booming demand for AI infrastructure to drive years of rapid profit growth, but recent earnings updates failed to justify some of the market's most optimistic expectations.

The selloff spread globally, dragging down Asian markets heavily exposed to the chip industry. Some strategists said the decline reflected an overdue pullback after months of rapid gains, while others pointed to crowded hedge-fund positions that amplified the move.

Bonds also struggled. Strong employment data reinforced the view that the Fed may need to keep policy tight to contain inflation pressures, particularly after the Iran conflict pushed energy prices higher. The two-year Treasury yield rose above 4.16%, while the benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/US10Y]) climbed past 4.5%, adding pressure to stocks and pushing mortgage rates higher.

Despite the turbulence, some investors argued the reaction was excessive. Economic growth remains resilient, employers continue to hire and many AI-related companies are still delivering strong revenue growth. Goldman Sachs analysts said they would view the recent weakness in Broadcom (AVGO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/AVGO]) shares as a buying opportunity because of the company's long-term growth prospects.

Another challenge for markets is a surge in new stock issuance. SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]), Anthropic (ANTHRO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ANTHRO]) and OpenAI (OPENAI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OPENAI]) are all expected to pursue public listings, while Alphabet (GOOG [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/GOOG])(GOOGL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/GOOGL]) recently surprised investors by announcing plans to raise $85 billion through equity sales. The influx of new shares could test investor demand and potentially dilute existing shareholders.

For investors, the week ahead may determine whether Friday's selloff was a temporary shakeout or the start of a more sustained reassessment of risk across financial markets.

title: Bybit launches tokenized IPO platform with SpaceX debut
content:
[NFT concept. 3d render illustration.]
Ivan Kuchin/iStock via Getty Images

Bybit, one of the world's largest cryptocurrency exchanges, said Sunday it will begin offering [https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/bybit-launches-ipo-express-becoming-one-of-first-centralized-crypto-exchanges-to-offer-tokenized-ipo-access-starting-with-spacex-302793370.html] retail investors access to tokenized initial public offerings, starting with SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]), in a move that highlights growing efforts by crypto companies to expand beyond digital asset trading and into traditional financial markets.

The exchange said its new "IPO Express" platform will allow eligible users to subscribe to tokenized representations of IPO shares at the offering price through their Bybit accounts. The service is being launched through a partnership with xStocks, a tokenized securities platform operated by Payward Services.

The announcement comes as investors await what could become one of the largest and most closely watched stock market debuts in history. At its target offering of $135 a share, SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) is seeking a roughly $75 billion capital raise, making the offering the largest IPO ever attempted and valuing the Elon Musk-led company at about $1.77 trillion.

Under Bybit's proposed timeline, eligible users can register interest and submit subscription requests for tokenized SpaceX shares through June 11. Allocations are scheduled for June 11-12, with tokenized shares expected to begin trading on Bybit's spot market on June 12.

The launch underscores the growing convergence between cryptocurrency platforms and traditional capital markets as exchanges seek new revenue streams and broader user engagement. Crypto firms have increasingly moved into services such as tokenized stocks, real-world assets and payment products as trading volumes in digital assets fluctuate.

For investors, the initiative illustrates how tokenization is emerging as a potentially significant growth area within financial markets. By allowing securities to be represented on blockchain networks, tokenization could expand access to investment products, increase trading flexibility and create new sources of transaction revenue for exchanges, brokers and infrastructure providers. The trend has attracted interest from both crypto-native companies and major financial institutions exploring blockchain-based settlement and custody systems.

Historically, access to high-profile IPO allocations has been largely limited to institutional investors, wealthy clients and customers of major brokerage firms. Retail investors often have been unable to participate until shares begin trading publicly, after initial allocations have been distributed and prices have started to move.

Bybit said tokenized IPO shares distributed through the xStocks framework will be backed one-for-one by underlying equity securities held in regulated broker-dealer custody. The company said allocations will be determined on a pro-rata basis if demand exceeds available supply.

The exchange cautioned that IPO-related assets may experience substantial volatility after listing and noted that offerings could be delayed, modified or canceled because of market or regulatory developments.

The launch follows a broader industry push into tokenized real-world assets, a market that includes tokenized stocks, bonds, funds and other traditional financial instruments. Industry advocates argue the approach could eventually allow securities to trade around the clock and integrate with blockchain-based financial applications, though regulatory frameworks remain under development in many jurisdictions.

title: Morningstar drops bombshell message on SpaceX IPO
content:
The hype around SpaceX’s blockbuster IPO has been near-impossible to ignore.

The company set a fixed price of $135 per share, is targeting a record $75 billion raise on the Nasdaq under the ticker SPCX, and is scheduled to debut on June 12, making it the largest initial public offering in history, more than double Saudi Aramco’s 2019 record of $29 billion.

But one of Wall Street’s most respected independent research firms just issued a warning that investors should not overlook.

Morningstar initiated its first-ever coverage of SpaceX this week and arrived at a fair value estimate of $780 billion, roughly 55% below the company’s $1.75 trillion IPO target.

The firm’s lead equity analyst, Nicolas Owens, didn’t soften the message: “We think the company has been significantly overvalued and investors will have opportunities to buy the stock at more attractive levels after the IPO,” CNBC reports.

Morningstar’s math: where the $780 billion comes from

Owens built his valuation using a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model, a method that estimates a company’s value based on how much cash it is realistically expected to generate over time.

A DCF model works backward from future projections, discounting them to reflect uncertainty; a higher uncertainty means a lower present value.

He assigned SpaceX’s core launch business and Starlink satellite broadband unit a combined enterprise value of approximately $611 billion.

Starlink deserves much of that credit.

The satellite internet division posted $11.3 billion in revenue in 2025, a 50% year-over-year increase, with operating income exceeding $4.4 billion, TradingKey reports.

Related: Navellier to SpaceX buyers: wait for escape velocity

Morningstar also gave SpaceX credit for its launch dominance.

The company accounted for 83% of all mass sent to orbit from Earth in 2025 and has reduced launch costs per kilogram by more than 95%, Morningstar notes.

Owens then applied probability-weighted scenarios to SpaceX’s AI segment, the one acquired through its all-stock merger with Elon Musk’s xAI company in February 2026.

That deal was the largest corporate merger by valuation in history, creating a combined entity worth $1.25 trillion.

The AI component added only $170 billion to Morningstar’s total, producing a blended fair value of $780 billion.

The implication: Morningstar believes the market is pricing SpaceX’s AI ambitions far too generously, based on the actual evidence available today.

The three key reasons Morningstar sees AI as a drag:

xAI posted an operating loss of $6.36 billion in 2025, pulling SpaceX’s net loss for the year to $4.94 billion, despite $18.67 billion in combined revenue. Owens flagged orbital data centers, one of SpaceX’s headline AI ambitions, as carrying “high uncertainty regarding scientific and economic feasibility.” Morningstar concluded that it does not see Grok, xAI’s chatbot, as one of the leading AI labs competing against OpenAI and Anthropic today.

Story Continues

Morningstar warns SpaceX's $1.75 trillion IPO valuation may be 55% above fair value, urging investors to wait for a better entry point after the June 12 debut.Apu Gomes / Getty Images

What the valuation gap looks like in practice

At the $1.75 trillion IPO target, SpaceX would be trading at approximately 94 times its 2025 revenue of $18.67 billion, TechTimes reports.

For context, Nvidia, one of the most profitable technology companies in the world, trades at roughly 22 times trailing revenue.

Owens labeled SpaceX shares “overvalued in almost any scenario, at least in the near term.”

He noted that sustaining a $1.75 trillion valuation through 2030 would require annual revenues exceeding $100 billion, with compound annual growth above 40%, according to Goldman Sachs data cited by TradingKey.

Related: Blue Origin's explosion just made SpaceX even harder to catch

ARK Invest, led by Cathie Wood, holds a far more optimistic view.

Yahoo Finance previously reported that Wood projected SpaceX could reach a $2.5 trillion enterprise value by 2030, calling the IPO target “grounded in a plausible trajectory” for Starlink, Starship, and orbital AI.

That is a direct disagreement between two credible sources, and investors need to decide which framework fits their risk tolerance.

Governance risk compounds the picture

Morningstar’s concerns stretch beyond the balance sheet.

Owens flagged Musk’s expected 85% voting control through a dual-class share structure, meaning retail investors who buy SPCX will have virtually no say in how the company is run.

More Space Stocks:

SpaceX’s long-anticipated IPO filing is out now, and it’s full of red flags Jim Cramer has bold new take on Elon Musk’s SpaceX IPO BofA raises red flag on SpaceX, OpenAI IPOs

The xAI merger was also not conducted at arm’s length.

Musk both negotiated and approved a deal that transferred his own AI company into SpaceX, a governance structure Morningstar flagged as a “material threat of value destruction.”

Danish pension fund AkademikerPension placed SpaceX on its investment blacklist, stating that SpaceX’s governance structure is “catastrophic,” Bloomberg reports.

What happens to the price after the IPO

Here is where Morningstar’s note becomes practically useful for ordinary investors.

The firm acknowledged that SpaceX’s stock is likely to hold steady or even climb in the days immediately following the listing.

But this is for reasons that have nothing to do with the company’s underlying value.

According to Yahoo Finance, SpaceX is offering only about 4% of its shares to the public in this IPO, an unusually small float.

Nasdaq’s fast-entry rules also make SPCX eligible for Nasdaq-100 index inclusion after just 15 trading days, which would force passive funds to buy the stock automatically, regardless of valuation.

Limited supply plus forced demand equals short-term price support.

The real test comes months later.

SpaceX’s lockup structure is notably different from a standard IPO.

Rather than a single 180-day lockup for insiders, the prospectus allows existing shareholders to sell 20% of their holdings as early as the first quarterly earnings report.

Selling rights then step up at 70, 90, 105, 120, and 135 days post-IPO, Fortune reports.

That staggered release means the float will expand steadily through the end of 2026, creating windows where sellers could outpace buyers.

Owens’ guidance: wait for the post-IPO excitement to settle and watch for the stock to drift toward a more defensible valuation as additional supply enters the market.

Before buying SPCX on day one, consider these signals to watch:

Starship payload delivery timeline: SpaceX’s prospectus targets commercial launches in the second half of 2026; delays would pressure the growth thesis. xAI revenue visibility: Until xAI posts operating profit, the $170 billion AI valuation Morningstar assigned rests on probabilities, not performance. Lockup expiration dates: The first insider sell window opens with second-quarter earnings, likely in August, and that is where the real price discovery begins.

Investing personality Jim Cramer, who addressed the SpaceX deal on CNBC’s “Mad Money,” said he “cannot fully sign off” on the deal at the current valuation, despite seeing three potential near-term catalysts.

Bank of America strategist Michael Hartnett has also raised concerns, warning that SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic landing in the same quarter could push tech concentration in the S&P 500past the 48% threshold that has historically preceded market bubbles.

Morningstar’s $780 billion fair value is not a prediction that SpaceX will fail.

It is a methodical argument that the gap between the company’s current fundamentals and its IPO price is too wide to ignore.

Long-term investors who buy before the excitement subsides may be paying today for returns that belong to 2030, and only if everything goes right.

That is a bet worth sizing carefully.

Related: Mark Cuban drops bold $500K email bet on SpaceX competitor

This story was originally published by TheStreet on Jun 7, 2026, where it first appeared in the Investing section. Add TheStreet as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

View Comments

title: AI IPO wave could test Wall Street's appetite for new shares
content:
[SpaceX Falcon Heavy Launch of Europa Clipper]
Brandon Moser

A surge of stock offerings tied to the artificial intelligence boom is poised to deliver trillions of dollars of new equity to U.S. markets, prompting investors to weigh whether demand can keep pace with one of the largest bursts of share issuance in recent memory, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

Highly anticipated public listings from SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]), OpenAI (OPENAI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OPENAI]) and Anthropic (ANTHRO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ANTHRO]), along with potential stock sales by established technology companies seeking capital for AI infrastructure, could dramatically expand the supply of publicly traded shares over the next several quarters.

The trend is significant for investors because it could reshape capital flows across the market. While the new offerings may create fresh opportunities to invest directly in leading AI companies, they could also draw money away from existing AI-related stocks, pressure valuations and increase volatility as indexes and institutional portfolios adjust.

The coming wave arrives as enthusiasm for AI remains a dominant force in equity markets. Semiconductor stocks have surged over the past year, while companies ranging from networking providers to legacy technology firms have benefited from investor expectations that AI spending will continue to accelerate.

Still, some market participants are questioning whether investor demand can absorb such a large volume of new stock issuance in a relatively short period. Ano Kuhanathan, head of corporate research at Allianz Trade, described the expected influx of shares as an unusually large supply event that has few historical parallels.

Concerns about valuation have also begun to surface. The Nasdaq 100 recently suffered its sharpest one-day decline in more than a year, while reports that Meta Platforms may consider a major equity raise weighed on its shares.

Despite those concerns, many strategists believe the market has sufficient liquidity to absorb both large IPOs and secondary stock offerings from companies funding AI-related investments. Nicholas Colas, co-founder of DataTrek Research, said investor capital remains abundant and should be capable of supporting both new listings and capital raises by existing public companies.

One factor that may limit immediate market disruption is that many of the expected IPOs plan to sell only a small percentage of their outstanding shares. SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]), for example, is expected to float only a modest portion of its equity, reducing the initial supply available for trading.

That dynamic may change over time. Historically, large IPOs that debut with relatively small public floats often see a substantial increase in tradable shares as insider lockups expire and early investors begin selling portions of their holdings. Goldman Sachs strategists estimate that could eventually result in roughly $1 trillion of additional equity entering the market by 2027.

Index inclusion could further complicate the picture. Recent changes by Nasdaq and FTSE Russell may allow newly public AI companies to join major benchmarks more quickly, creating automatic demand from index funds and exchange-traded funds that track those gauges.

At the same time, passive managers would likely need to reduce holdings in existing constituents to accommodate new entrants, potentially creating selling pressure elsewhere in the market. Research Affiliates founder Rob Arnott said the gradual release of additional shares over time could create persistent pressure on index weightings and valuations.

Some investors believe the most significant effect may be on companies that have benefited from serving as proxies for privately held AI leaders. Because investors previously had no direct way to buy shares of OpenAI (OPENAI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OPENAI]) or Anthropic (ANTHRO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ANTHRO]), many instead purchased stakes in suppliers, partners and related technology firms.

That trade could begin to unwind once investors gain direct access to the companies at the center of the AI boom.

Nigel Green, chief investment officer at DeVere Group, said investors who spent years buying indirect exposure to leading AI startups may eventually rotate capital into the companies themselves. As a result, some of the scarcity premium enjoyed by AI-adjacent stocks could diminish.

The shift could affect a broad range of beneficiaries of the AI rally, including chipmakers Nvidia and Broadcom, as well as companies supplying infrastructure and custom silicon to AI developers.

Tesla may also face a new competitive dynamic. For years, the electric-vehicle maker has served as the primary public-market vehicle for investors seeking exposure to Elon Musk. With SpaceX preparing to trade publicly, some investors may choose to shift capital toward Musk's rocket and satellite business, particularly because it also houses artificial intelligence venture xAI.

Whether investors ultimately embrace the new offerings may depend on their willingness to overlook steep valuations and ongoing losses. SpaceX, for example, reported a multibillion-dollar operating loss last year and is seeking a valuation that implies a rich multiple of revenue.

For now, investor enthusiasm appears strong. Demand for SpaceX shares has reportedly exceeded supply. Still, market strategists caution that expectations surrounding AI companies are exceptionally high, leaving little margin for operational missteps once the excitement of their public debuts fades.

As Anthony Saglimbene, chief market strategist at Ameriprise, noted, the companies entering public markets are doing so at enormous valuations and under intense scrutiny, a combination that could make investors far less patient if growth falls short of expectations, Bloomberg News reported.

MORE ON SPACEX, ANTHROPIC, ETC.

 	* SpaceX IPO: Boom, Bloodbath, Or Both [https://seekingalpha.com/article/4912497-spacex-ipo-boom-bloodbath-or-both]
 	* SpaceX IPO: 2 Reasons To Consider Indirect Ownership Via Alphabet (Downgrade) [https://seekingalpha.com/article/4912488-spacex-ipo-2-reasons-to-consider-indirect-ownership-via-alphabet-downgrade]
 	* OpenAI plans major ChatGPT overhaul ahead of expected IPO, FT reports [https://seekingalpha.com/news/4601226-openai-plans-major-chatgpt-overhaul-ahead-of-expected-ipo-ft-reports]
 	* SpaceX IPO positioned for an oversubscription ‌rate of two times: Reuters [https://seekingalpha.com/news/4601213-spacex-ipo-over-two-times-oversubscribed]

title: Fidelity slashed its SpaceX minimum to $2,000 for retail investors — but the fine print can ban you from IPOs for life
content:
For most of history, getting into a marquee IPO at the offer price was a privilege reserved for big accounts. Fidelity just changed that for SpaceX, opening the offering to any customer with at least $2,000 in a retail brokerage account, a fraction of the threshold that has historically gated access to hot new issues. (1)

That's an easy in. The costly out is in the fine print: a 15-calendar-day leash, and penalties that escalate with each early sale until a third strike triggers a lifetime ban on Fidelity IPOs tied to your SSN.

Must Read

Robert Kiyosaki says this 1 asset will surge 400% in a year and begs investors not to miss this ‘explosion’ Prime US real estate was a rich person's game — then something changed. Now everyday Americans are getting a piece of the action for as little as $100 Millionaires under 43 are reshaping investing — just 25% of their portfolios are in stocks. Here’s where their money is going

Why Fidelity dropped its SpaceX IPO minimum to $2,000

Fidelity is reacting to the major jump in supply. Most IPOs reserve only 5% to 10% of the total offering for retail customers, which restricts the shares that brokers like Fidelity can allocate to everyday clients. For SpaceX, Fidelity says the company has decided to reserve up to 30% of the offering, which is why, in its words, it "decided to reduce IPO eligibility for this offering." (1)

Fidelity tied the lower bar to this SpaceX offering specifically, and has not adopted it as a permanent policy. It reviews eligibility on a per-IPO basis. Don't assume the $2,000 door stays open for the next big name — like Anthropic or OpenAI. (1)

Fidelity's flipping rule: sell SpaceX in 15 days and you're banned

If you're allocated SpaceX shares (limited to begin with) and you sell within the first 15 calendar days from the start of secondary-market trading, Fidelity will label you as a "flipper," and that restricts your access to future new-issue equity offerings through Fidelity. The first day you can sell without the label is the 16th calendar day after the stock starts trading.

The penalties escalate with each offense (1):

First flip: blocked from future IPOs for six months Second flip: blocked for one year Third flip: permanently banned, tied to your SSN

FINRA defines "flipping" as a sale within the first 30 days of an offering, and brokers set their own penalty structures around that benchmark. The ban you'd face is Fidelity's policy, not a regulatory one. (2)

You can technically sell — Fidelity doesn't freeze the shares during the 15-day window — but doing so starts you up the penalty ladder. The retail hype for SpaceX could amplify the regular volatility that comes with new tickers, so a slide inside the window forces a choice: hold and ride it out, or sell and eat both the loss and the penalty.

Story Continues

One early sale costs you six months, not forever. The lifetime ban takes three strikes. Of course, with OpenAI, Anthropic and other big names lining up to go public, investors may not want to risk it.

What the $2,000 SpaceX IPO access really gets you

It’s important to know that clearing the $2,000 bar doesn't guarantee you shares either. Fidelity says it will try to allocate some shares to every customer who confirms interest, but expects demand to outstrip the supply it receives. If it can't fill everyone, it runs a lottery, meaning you could get a partial fill, or nothing at all. (1) Retail investors often get shut out of hot IPOs since allocations are limited, and the price has often already run up by the open.

Insiders aren't on the same leash. SpaceX reserved up to 5% of the offering for a directed-share program, shares for "certain employees and persons" its executives pick, with no lockup at all. They bought at $135 and can sell on day one, roughly $3.75 billion in stock that can hit the market immediately while retail waits 15 days. (3) One early investor’s 7% stake is set to be worth close to $90 billion.

Read More: BlackRock warns buying and holding the S&P 500 isn’t enough for retirement anymore — here's why

SpaceX IPO date, price and how big the $75 billion raise is

SpaceX filed its first prospectus with the SEC on May 20, 2026, then amended it on June 1 to list Class A shares on Nasdaq and the new Nasdaq Texas exchange under the ticker NASDAQ:SPCX, at a fixed $135 per share. None of this is locked in just yet — finalized details are expected after the close on June 11, with the trading circus set to begin on June 12. (3)

SpaceX skipped the usual step of marketing a price range and narrowing it through bookbuilding, going straight to a single fixed number.

SpaceX floated a figure above $2 trillion to investors in April, according to Bloomberg. By late May, Bloomberg reported the target had settled to at least $1.8 trillion after consultations with advisers and investors — a number Musk publicly disputed, replying "False" on X to a post citing the report. (4) The fixed price now implies a valuation near $1.75 trillion, below what he disputed. (3) Not everyone buys the math: Morningstar pegs SpaceX's fair value at roughly $780 billion, less than half the target.

SpaceX reported $18.67 billion in 2025 revenue, up sharply as Starlink subscriptions and launch services compounded, against a 2025 operating loss of roughly $2.59 billion as the company poured capital into Starship and a fast-growing AI segment built around its xAI acquisition. (3)

At $135 a share, SpaceX is selling 555.6 million shares for a raise of roughly $75 billion, with underwriters holding an option to buy 83.33 million more at the same price. (3) That would dwarf the current record. Saudi Aramco's 2019 listing raised $25.6 billion at IPO, a figure that rose to $29.4 billion once it exercised its over-allotment option. (5) Aramco's record isn't even in the same galaxy — at $75 billion, SpaceX would land as the largest IPO in history.

Should you buy the SpaceX IPO through Fidelity?

If you opt in, plan to hold past day 15, treat an allocation as a maybe rather than a sure thing, and size the position to what you can afford to leave alone. Fidelity's 15-day window is the shortest of the brokers offering SpaceX, well under FINRA's 30-day benchmark, though it pairs that with some of the steepest penalties, including the SSN-tied lifetime ban.

There's also a way to get exposure without touching the IPO at all. Index providers rewrote their fast-entry rules for giant listings, and SpaceX is set to land in most major US indexes within weeks — CRSP-based funds like Vanguard's VTI in as little as five trading days, the Russell 1000 about a week out, the Nasdaq-100 at 15. The S&P 500 is the lone holdout, after keeping its rules unchanged. So if you hold a broad index fund or a 401(k), you may end up owning a slice of SpaceX without ever placing the trade.

And if your choice is to sit on the sidelines and watch the rocket launch, get some popcorn ready. This one's for the history books.

title: How to trade the AI IPO boom without chasing the hype
content:
[IPO Concept with Stock Market Graphs]
AlexSecret

Wall Street's excitement over upcoming IPOs such as SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]), Anthropic (ANTHRO [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/ANTHRO]) and OpenAI (OPENAI [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/OPENAI]) has investors eager to gain exposure to the next phase of the artificial intelligence boom. But history suggests caution may be warranted, Investor’s Business Daily reported Saturday.

Research cited in _The Lifecycle Trade_ found that more than 90% of IPOs eventually trade below their first-day low, highlighting the risks of buying immediately after a stock begins trading.

One strategy used by growth investors is to wait for an "IPO base" to form. This is a period, typically lasting one to five weeks after a company's debut, when shares settle into a relatively narrow trading range following their initial volatility. The pattern can help identify whether institutional investors are building positions and establishing support for the stock.

An IPO base usually begins after shares reach an early post-listing peak and then pull back. If the stock later rises above that initial high on strong trading volume, some investors view it as a potential buy signal. Heavy volume can indicate that large institutional investors are driving demand.

Google's (GOOG [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/GOOG])(GOOGL [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/GOOGL]) 2004 market debut is often cited as a successful example. After reaching an early high, the stock formed a short base, then broke out on strong volume and climbed sharply in the following weeks. More recently, AI infrastructure company CoreWeave (CRWV [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/CRWV]) posted substantial gains after emerging from a five-week IPO base in 2025.

The approach is not foolproof. Spotify (SPOT [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPOT]) formed a similar pattern after its 2018 debut, but institutional buying failed to materialize and the breakout stalled. The stock later fell sharply before recovering years afterward.

Investors looking at newly public companies often watch for several signs of strength during the base-building process: shares holding above the IPO price, weekly closes near the upper end of the trading range and declining trading volume during pullbacks. Together, those signals can suggest that selling pressure is easing while demand remains intact.

Waiting for an IPO base will not eliminate risk, but it can help investors avoid the sharp swings that frequently accompany a company's first days as a public stock and provide a more disciplined entry point.

title: Standard Chartered CIO Warns Mega AI IPO Wave Could Create Near-Term Market Headwinds
content:
spacex dragon capsule clouds NEW SIZE ©NASA Johnson

The planned stock market debuts of Anthropic, OpenAI and SpaceX (NASDAQ:SPCX) could create short-term pressure on financial markets as investors absorb a large volume of new equity offerings, according to Steve Brice, Global Chief Investment Officer for Wealth Solutions at Standard Chartered.

Speaking on CNBC’s “Access Middle East” on Tuesday, Brice said the scale of the upcoming listings may create temporary challenges for market participants.

“There’s going to be some digestion challenges of these IPOs coming through the market, and some of the broadening out usually does take place well, but it doesn’t necessarily happen in a totally smooth fashion,” Brice said.

While he remains constructive on the broader outlook, Brice indicated that he is taking a more cautious stance in the near term.

Summer Weakness Remains a Possibility

Brice noted that markets could experience periods of volatility or weakness during the coming months, particularly as investors navigate both the IPO pipeline and ongoing geopolitical developments.

“I isn’t ‘super, super bullish at this point.'”

“We could see some weakness at some point over the summer months, and maybe that would fit into what’s happening in the Middle East as well,” he said.

Despite those concerns, Brice suggested that any market pullback could eventually present opportunities for long-term investors.

Potential Buying Opportunities Beyond Near-Term Volatility

According to Brice, temporary market weakness should not necessarily be viewed negatively by investors with a longer investment horizon.

Over time, he said, any decline triggered by IPO-related selling pressure or macroeconomic uncertainty could create attractive entry points into the market.

The comments come as investors prepare for what could be one of the largest periods of technology-related public offerings in recent history, with several AI-focused companies expected to seek valuations measured in the hundreds of billions of dollars.

U.S. Economic Data Could Support Sentiment

Brice also pointed to upcoming U.S. employment figures as a potential source of support for financial markets.

He said expectations for relatively healthy labor market data could help sustain investor confidence in the near term, although that support may prove temporary if disruptions to global energy supplies persist.

According to Brice, one key risk remains the continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Should the strategic shipping route remain inaccessible through the end of the summer, economic conditions could deteriorate as energy-related pressures intensify.

Story Continues

Energy Supply Concerns Remain a Major Risk

Oil prices have surged amid the ongoing standoff between the United States and Iran, with the Trump administration maintaining a blockade on Iranian ports while Tehran continues to keep the Strait of Hormuz closed.

Brice warned that the impact extends well beyond crude oil markets.

“Inventories are being run down at a rapid pace, not just in physical crude, but also across petrochemicals or urea and different inputs into the production cycle around the world,” Brice said.

The depletion of inventories across multiple industrial supply chains could eventually place additional pressure on economic growth and inflation, making energy markets a critical factor for investors to monitor in the months ahead.

title: SA Asks: Will Tesla merge with SpaceX?
content:
[Tesla electric car retail store and company brand logo sign]

Last week, Wedbush analyst Dan Ives said his firm expects a post-IPO SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) to merge with Tesla (TSLA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TSLA]) next year, putting the odds of a merger at over 80%.

“Musk wants to own and control more of the AI ecosystem,” said Ives, according to Barron's [https://www.barrons.com/articles/spacex-tesla-merger-elon-musk-79229b32]. “The holy grail could be combining SpaceX and Tesla in some way to give the connected tissue between both disruptive tech stalwarts [that are] looking to lead the AI Revolution in this next tech chapter for the market.”

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts Joseph L. Shaefer [https://seekingalpha.com/author/joseph-l-shaefer] and Multiplo Invest [https://seekingalpha.com/author/multiplo-invest] if they see Tesla (TSLA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TSLA]) merging with SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]), also known as Space Exploration Technologies.

Joseph L. Shaefer [https://seekingalpha.com/author/joseph-l-shaefer]: Merging SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) with Tesla (TSLA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TSLA]) is a brilliant idea—at least for Elon Musk. For the shareholders of each? That remains to be seen. 

Tesla sales have been declining in the U.S. since federal EV subsidies were withdrawn. In Europe and China, Tesla is now a second fiddle to BYD (BYDDF [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BYDDF]) in sales. SpaceX, on the other hand, is filled with near-infinite possibilities.

So why would Mr. Musk want to merge the two? Possibly because while he holds about 85% of the voting control at SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]), he only has a 12% to 15% lock on Tesla (TSLA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TSLA]). It would put Mr. Musk in complete control of two large companies in different industries. There is possibly some synergy as well, given the forward-thinking engineering in some Tesla automobiles.

But _will_ SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) merge with Tesla (TSLA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TSLA])? I don’t think so. A merger of SpaceX, with (currently) low revenue and poor margins, and Tesla, a declining-in-sales but still very profitable company, would have the _Teslarati_ in an uproar if they suddenly owned a different company than they signed on for. 

Also, potential investors beyond Tesla would likely be concerned about key-man risk and a board that might just be a rubber stamp.

Finally, regulators across the spectrum would likely be wary of an organization that, for instance, could derive some income from classified projects and then use that knowledge for profit elsewhere in the conglomerate.

If this happens, it won’t be any time soon!

Multiplo Invest [https://seekingalpha.com/author/multiplo-invest]: The possibility of a merger between Tesla (TSLA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TSLA]) and SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) is real. After all, Elon Musk doesn't have the same decision-making power at Tesla that he does at SpaceX. At SpaceX, Musk owns 85.1% of the company pre-IPO and is expected to remain a reference shareholder if the IPO moves forward. The merger would likely restore Musk's voting power in all his businesses.

The merger would also create a new narrative for Tesla, which is currently struggling to show strong results as it once did.

Finally, the merger would have two additional consequences. Musk would become the world's first trillionaire (if the SpaceX IPO isn't enough). The merger would also create the fifth company with the most bitcoins in the world, given that Tesla (TSLA [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/TSLA]) has 11,509 bitcoins (BTC-USD [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/BTC-USD]) and SpaceX (SPCX [https://seekingalpha.com/symbol/SPCX]) has 18,712 bitcoins. The overlapping of businesses favors the operation.
